items.description: Public struct/class Greeter with greet(name: String) -> String returning "Hello, <name>!" and greet() -> String returning "Hello, world!"

rules_checked.reasoning: Item declares kind 'type' and describes a public struct/class Greeter with two methods (greet(name:) and greet()). This is genuinely a type declaration, not a function, protocol, or config change — the declared kind matches what is being built.

Target Sources/Greeter/Greeter.swift lives inside the Greeter target defined in Package.swift ('Greeter' target). The package currently only contains a placeholder namespace enum (GreeterModule.swift); placing the concrete Greeter type in the Greeter module is the correct, indeed only sensible, home given the package is purpose-built for this feature.

This is a standalone utility package with no view/view-model/service layering present (Package.swift shows only a Greeter target and its tests). The Greeter type is a plain data/logic type performing simple string formatting — there is no view-layer domain logic or data access from a view model being smuggled in, so this altitude concern does not surface a violation.

No existing type currently implements greeting behavior — Sources/Greeter/GreeterModule.swift is only a namespace-marker enum with no members. Introducing a new Greeter type in its own file (one type per file) is warranted and matches SRP rather than overloading the placeholder namespace or an unrelated existing type.

The type name 'Greeter' is a plain noun naming the concept, and greet(name:) / greet() are verb-form method names with a clear argument label, consistent with Swift API Design Guidelines (no Hungarian/get- prefixes, argument label reads naturally: greet(name: "Ada")).

The item introduces only a concrete struct/class with no protocol. No speculative abstraction is being added, so there is nothing to justify via a substitution/test-seam need — rule is satisfied by absence of any protocol.

The description introduces no imports or dependencies at all — a self-contained type with pure string-returning methods. No forbidden import or dependency-direction violation is possible from this description.
